questions.text: What does a Kubernetes Deployment manage?
questions.explanation: A Deployment manages ReplicaSets. When you update a Deployment (e.g., change the image), it creates a new ReplicaSet for the new version and scales it up while scaling the old one down. This is what enables rolling updates. The Deployment keeps history of ReplicaSets for rollbacks.

questions.text: What is 'maxUnavailable: 0' in a RollingUpdate strategy?

options.text: No existing Pods can be removed until a new Pod is ready
The update pauses if any Pod fails

questions.explanation: maxUnavailable: 0 means zero Pods from the desired count can be unavailable during the update. Combined with maxSurge: 1, new Pods are added before old ones are removed, ensuring no capacity loss. This is a 'blue/green-style' rolling update safe for production traffic.

questions.text: Which Service type makes a service accessible only within the Kubernetes cluster?
questions.explanation: ClusterIP is the default Service type, providing a stable internal IP accessible only within the cluster. It's used for service-to-service communication. NodePort exposes on all node IPs at a high port; LoadBalancer creates an external cloud load balancer.

questions.text: How does a Service find which Pods to route traffic to?

options.text: By label selectors matching Pod labels

questions.explanation: Services use label selectors to find backing Pods. If spec.selector.app: nginx, the Service routes to all Pods with label app=nginx in the same namespace. This decoupling means Services don't need to know about Deployments — they just match labels.

questions.text: What kubectl command rolls back a Deployment to its previous version?
questions.explanation: kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name> rolls back to the previous revision. Add --to-revision=N to roll back to a specific revision number. View revision history with kubectl rollout history deployment/<name>. Each image update creates a new revision.

questions.text: What is the purpose of spec.selector.matchLabels in a Deployment?

options.text: Selects which Service routes to this Deployment
Specifies which Pods the Deployment manages (must match template labels)
Selects which Nodes the Pods can run on
Filters which images can be used in the Deployment

questions.explanation: spec.selector.matchLabels defines which Pods the Deployment (and its ReplicaSet) owns and manages. It must match the labels in spec.template.metadata.labels. This selector is immutable after creation. The Deployment controller uses this to count existing Pods and create/delete as needed.

questions.text: What port range is used for NodePort Services?
questions.explanation: NodePort services are allocated ports in the 30000-32767 range by default (configurable in kube-apiserver with --service-node-port-range). You can specify a fixed NodePort within this range or let Kubernetes assign one. The service is accessible at <NodeIP>:<NodePort>.

questions.text: What is 'kubectl rollout status deployment/my-deploy' used for?

options.text: Waits for and reports the completion status of a rolling update

questions.explanation: kubectl rollout status watches and reports the progress of a rolling update, returning 0 on success or 1 on failure/timeout. Use it in CI/CD pipelines to wait for deployments to complete: kubectl rollout status deployment/app --timeout=5m. It exits when all new Pods are Ready.

questions.text: What Kubernetes resource automatically scales Deployments based on CPU usage?
questions.explanation: HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA) automatically scales the number of Pod replicas based on CPU utilization, memory usage, or custom metrics. It reads metrics from the Metrics Server and adjusts the Deployment's replica count. ClusterAutoscaler scales the number of nodes; VPA adjusts resource requests.

questions.text: What is the Recreate deployment strategy?

options.text: Creates new Pods before deleting old ones
Terminates all existing Pods before creating new ones (causes downtime)
Rolls back to the previous deployment

questions.explanation: The Recreate strategy terminates all existing Pods before creating new ones, causing downtime during the transition. Use it when your application cannot run multiple versions simultaneously (e.g., database schema migrations that are incompatible with old code). RollingUpdate is preferred for zero-downtime deployments.
